notes: 80 lines (1.67x new skill). Recent changes lists 7 line-level diffs (all git-discoverable). Learnings items 2-4 explain standard Go patterns that are self-evident from reading the code. Artifacts section repeats file list from Tasks.
All 10 assertions pass for both configurations across all 3 evals — assertions are non-discriminating and test minimum viability only
New skill outputs average 44 lines vs 82.7 lines for old skill (47% reduction) — the conciseness philosophy is clearly effective
New skill uses 6 frontmatter fields vs old skill's 12 — old skill includes noise fields (researcher, topic, tags, status, last_updated, last_updated_by, type) that add no value for AI-to-AI handoffs
Old skill consistently produces redundant 'Artifacts' section that repeats information already in Tasks and Working Set sections
Old skill's 'Recent changes' section lists git-discoverable diffs — directly contradicts the new skill's philosophy of 'include only what the next session can't discover from the filesystem'

New skill's Critical Context + Working Set distinction (must-read-first vs will-touch-later) is a structural improvement not present in old skill — old skill's 'Critical References' is a flat list without urgency framing

New skill's Learnings sections tend toward actionable insights ('write-through pattern means invalidation must happen on write operations') vs old skill's explanatory style ('The cache uses a write-through pattern where Set() is called on read-miss')
New skill completes faster (30.3s avg vs 35.0s avg) — likely because init-handoff.sh automates frontmatter generation that old skill agents construct manually

To create discriminating assertions for future iterations, consider: frontmatter field count <= 7, document under 60 lines, no Artifacts section, no Recent changes section, Critical Context present with urgency framing
